summary: A 21-year-old student drowned at Tamolitch Blue Pool on June 26. The pool's 37-degree water and cliff jumping risks make it one of Oregon's most dangerous swimming holes.
title: A Loss at Blue Pool, and a Reminder for Everyone Who Loves It
body:
Tamolitch Blue Pool is one of the most beloved spots within reach of our community. The short hike through old growth forest, the unreal sapphire color of the water, the quiet of the McKenzie River canyon: it is the kind of place people drive over the mountains to see. It is also one of the most dangerous swimming holes in Oregon, and last week it claimed another life.

On the evening of Friday, June 26, Kenny Truong, a 21-year-old Wichita State University student from Wichita, Kansas, drowned after jumping into the pool. According to the Linn County Sheriff's Office, dispatchers received a 911 call at about 8:47 p.m. reporting that a man had gone into the water off Highway 126 and could not get out. Truong was visiting the area with friends. Witnesses said he struggled as he swam toward shore, then slipped beneath the surface. People at the scene tried to help, and crews from the Linn County and Lane County Sheriff's Offices and Upper McKenzie Rural Fire Protection District responded, but he could not be revived. A fundraiser to support his family had been started in the days that followed.

Our hearts go out to his family and friends. We share what follows not to sensationalize a tragedy, but because the same conditions that make Blue Pool breathtaking are the ones that make it deadly, and knowing them ahead of time saves lives.

## Why Blue Pool Is So Dangerous

The water is brutally cold. It averages around 37 degrees year round, cold enough to trigger what experts call cold water shock. The National Weather Service notes that this response, an involuntary gasp followed by rapid, uncontrollable breathing, can begin in water below 70 degrees. It can overwhelm even strong, experienced swimmers within seconds, before they ever reach shore.

The pool is also deeper than it looks (roughly 30 feet), and it sits at the base of cliffs that range from about 10 to 60 feet. Cliff jumping has caused serious injuries and deaths here over the years. And the setting that makes it feel so remote and peaceful is exactly what makes a rescue so hard: there is little to no cell service, the trail in runs a couple of miles into the wilderness, and it can take hours for help to arrive or for an injured person to reach a hospital.

## This Is Not a New Problem

Emergency crews respond to incidents at Tamolitch nearly every year. In 2013, University of Oregon tennis player Alex Rovello died in a diving and drowning accident at the falls. In 2015, a 52-year-old Bend man died after a fall of roughly 45 feet while taking photos near the pool. Just last month, in May 2026, a 53-year-old woman from Terrebonne had to be hoisted out by a Coast Guard helicopter and flown to a Springfield hospital after badly injuring her knee on the steep terrain, a rescue that pulled in multiple agencies from across two counties.

The risks have been serious enough that in 2023, Forest Service officials floated the idea of banning swimming at Blue Pool after five people were rescued there in a single three day stretch. That proposal did not move forward, and swimming remains legal. Legal, though, is not the same as safe.

## Before You Go: Blue Pool Safety

- **Respect the cold.** The water averages about 37 degrees. Sudden immersion can cause an involuntary gasp and rapid breathing that lead to drowning, even for good swimmers. Easing in is safer than jumping.
- **Know the depth and the cliffs.** The pool is around 30 feet deep and ringed by cliffs of roughly 10 to 60 feet. Cliff jumping has caused deaths and serious injuries.
- **Plan for no signal.** Cell reception is minimal to nonexistent. Help can take hours to reach you. Tell someone your plan before you go.
- **Go together, stay together.** There are no lifeguards. Keep an eye on the people you came with, and do not swim alone.
- **In an emergency, call 911 immediately.** At a location this remote, every minute matters.
- **Check conditions first.** For current information, contact the McKenzie River Ranger Station at (541) 822-3381.

If you have visited Blue Pool, you know why people love it. The best way to honor that, and the people we have lost there, is to treat it with the respect it demands.

summary: With two fires burning in Redmond over the same holiday weekend, it's a good moment to note that Redmond Fire and Rescue's district-wide burn ban remains active. Residents and business owners are asked to hold off on debris, weeds, or brush fires and report any smoke or flames promptly to 911.
title: District-Wide Burn Ban Remains in Effect Amid Holiday Fire Activity
body:
With two fires burning in Redmond over the same holiday weekend, it's a good moment to note that Redmond Fire and Rescue's district-wide burn ban remains active.

Residents and business owners handling debris, weeds, or brush on their property are asked to hold off until conditions ease and to report any smoke or flames promptly to 911.

summary: The City of Redmond's weekly traffic report lists several closures residents and business owners should plan around, including a crane operation on SW 9th Street, continued closures on NW Birch Avenue and NW 8th Street, and a street closure on NW Elm Avenue and NW 29th Street from July 10 through July 17.
title: Road Work and Closures to Plan Around This Week
body:
The City of Redmond's weekly traffic report lists several closures residents and business owners should plan around this week:

- A crane operation on SW 9th Street between Deschutes and Evergreen on July 9.
- Continued full closures on NW Birch Avenue and NW 8th Street tied to the Cedar and Birch improvement project.
- A street closure on NW Elm Avenue and NW 29th Street from July 10 through July 17 for utility work.

Full detour details are available through the city's Transportation office or at redmondstreets.com.

summary: Redmond's Independence Day parade brought crowds downtown Saturday morning for the community's long running holiday tradition, a nice reminder of how many neighbors and local businesses turn out to support it each year.
title: Redmond's Fourth of July Parade Brings Crowds Downtown
body: Central Oregon Daily reported that Redmond's Independence Day parade brought crowds downtown Saturday morning for the community's long running holiday tradition, a nice reminder of how many neighbors and local businesses turn out to support it each year.

summary: A fire on Saturday afternoon heavily damaged two homes in the 2700 block of NW Greenwood Avenue. One home was unoccupied and listed for sale, while a resident in the second home escaped safely after a passing neighbor alerted her to the flames.
title: Fire Heavily Damages Two Homes on NW Greenwood Avenue
body:
Separately on Saturday afternoon, a fire heavily damaged two homes in the 2700 block of NW Greenwood Avenue, according to the Redmond Spokesman and KTVZ. One home was unoccupied and listed for sale, while a resident in the second home escaped safely after a passing neighbor alerted her to the flames.

No injuries were reported, and Redmond Fire and Rescue Marshal Tom Mooney said the cause is still under investigation.

summary: A wildfire broke out Saturday evening in northwest Redmond, sending one neighborhood into a Level 3 'Go Now' evacuation and a wider area into a Level 2 'Get Set' warning. The Coyner Fire grew to about 11 acres before crews stopped its forward progress.
title: Coyner Fire Prompts Evacuations in NW Redmond Over Holiday Weekend
body:
A wildfire broke out Saturday evening in northwest Redmond, sending residents in one neighborhood into a Level 3 "Go Now" evacuation and giving others in a wider area a Level 2 "Get Set" warning. The Bulletin and the Redmond Spokesman reported the fire, dubbed the Coyner Fire, started around 8:30 p.m. near NW Tetherow Road and NW Helmholtz Way and grew to about 11 acres before crews stopped its forward progress.

Central Oregon Fire Information and KTVZ confirmed that interagency crews, including five engines, a hand crew, a water tender, and a dozer, worked the fire through the night, with powerlines and homes at risk in the area. A temporary evacuation point was set up at Highland Baptist Church on SW Highland Avenue, and the cause remains under investigation.

If you or your neighbors were in the evacuation zone, the City of Redmond and Deschutes County Sheriff's Office recommend checking deschutes.org/emergency or the Watch Duty app for the latest zone status before assuming it's safe to return.

summary: Fourth of July weekend brought parades, parties, and several fires to Central Oregon. Here is what happened around Redmond, Terrebonne, Crooked River Ranch, and the wider region.
title: Redmond Compass Roundup: July 6, 2026
body:
The Fourth of July weekend brought parades, parties, and unfortunately a few fires to Central Oregon. Here is what happened around Redmond, Terrebonne, Crooked River Ranch, and the wider region over the last day or so.

## This Week Around Redmond

The Coyner Fire that started Saturday evening in northwest Redmond has grown to about 20.5 acres and is now 10 percent contained, according to [KTVZ](https://ktvz.com/). Level 2 "Be Set" evacuations remain in place for the area near NW Tetherow Road and NW Helmholtz Way, meaning residents there should stay ready to leave at a moment's notice. The [Redmond Spokesman](https://redmondspokesman.com/) reports crews have seen no new fire growth since Sunday and are focused on mop up work, with Redmond Fire and Rescue Marshal Tom Mooney confirming no injuries so far. The cause remains under investigation.

Illegal fireworks sparked at least two residential fires in Redmond over the holiday weekend. [KTVZ](https://ktvz.com/) reports a manufactured home being used for storage near NW 4th Street and NW Hemlock Court caught fire Sunday evening after fireworks ignited nearby dry vegetation, though crews kept the flames from reaching neighboring homes. [The Bulletin](https://bendbulletin.com/) reported a separate fire damaged two homes near NW 28th and NW Greenwood on Saturday afternoon. Redmond Fire and Rescue is reminding everyone that all fireworks, legal or illegal, are banned within city limits, and anyone responsible for a fire can be held liable for the damage.

A small vegetation fire also broke out at the Deschutes County Fairgrounds in Redmond during Saturday night's fireworks celebrations, one of two fires reported near shows that night, according to [KTVZ](https://ktvz.com/). The other happened on Bend's Pilot Butte during that city's fireworks display, where crews quickly put out the flare up, a scene [Central Oregon Daily](https://www.centraloregondaily.com/) also captured on video. Officials had not confirmed by Sunday afternoon whether the Fairgrounds fire was tied to the celebrations.

Redmond kicked off the holiday weekend Saturday morning with its annual Fourth of July parade through downtown, a tradition that brought neighbors out to celebrate together, [Central Oregon Daily](https://www.centraloregondaily.com/) reports. [KTVZ](https://ktvz.com/) also covered the parade as part of its Independence Day coverage across the region, part of a bigger year of festivities tied to America's 250th birthday.

*It's all figure-out-able, even fire season. Stay safe out there, Redmond.*

summary: Men allegedly shot Roman candles at each other July 4, sparking a fire near a business with propane tanks. Bend police logged 85 firework complaint emails that week.
title: Two Bend Men Arrested After Roman Candle Fire
body:
Two Bend men were arrested July 4 after allegedly shooting Roman candles at each other and sparking a fire near a business with propane tanks nearby, Central Oregon Daily reports.

The Bulletin notes the incident was one of several fireworks related fires across the region that weekend, and that Bend police logged 85 firework complaint emails between June 30 and July 6, about normal for the holiday.

summary: Downtown Redmond's splash park closed Monday, July 6 after a chlorine leak. Crews waited on the HazMat team from Salem; no injuries reported.
title: Chlorine Leak Closes Centennial Park
body:
A chlorine leak closed Centennial Park in downtown Redmond on Monday, July 6.

The Bulletin reports Redmond police set up a perimeter around Seventh and Eighth streets by midmorning while city crews drained the splash park to address the leak.

Redmond Spokesman adds that the park stayed closed into the afternoon as crews waited on the Hazardous Materials Emergency Response Team from Salem, though no injuries were reported and a chlorine smell was the main sign anything was wrong.

summary: Fire burned within feet of homes northwest of Redmond over the holiday weekend, triggering a Level 3 'Go Now' evacuation. No homes were lost.
title: Coyner Fire Prompts Level 3 Evacuations Near Redmond
body:
The Coyner Fire that broke out northwest of Redmond over the holiday weekend burned within feet of homes and triggered a Level 3 "Go Now" evacuation Saturday night, according to Central Oregon Daily.

No homes were lost, and residents who spoke with the outlet described how close the flames came, with crews expected to remain on scene for several more days.

KTVZ reports the fire has held at 20.5 acres and is 10 percent contained, with Level 2 "Be Set" evacuations still in place for the area.

summary: A full accounting of Fourth of July weekend fires across Central Oregon, from Bend arrests to Redmond's vacant house fire and the Coyner Fire.
title: The Fourth That Kept Firefighters Busy
body:
Central Oregon is still sorting out the aftermath of a Fourth of July weekend with more than its share of fire calls. The Bulletin pulled together a full accounting of what burned across the region, and it is a useful reminder of just how much dry fuel and fireworks do not mix out here.

In Bend, two men were arrested July 4 after allegedly shooting Roman candles at each other and sparking a small fire near propane tanks, while a separate structure fire that same weekend destroyed a utility trailer and an RV. The Pilot Butte fireworks show caused its usual pair of small spot fires, both handled by crews already standing by.

Closer to home, Redmond absorbed the bulk of the damage. A low flying shell from the sanctioned Redmond fireworks show sparked a fire on Deschutes County Fair and Expo property that was out within the hour, and a firework ignited dry vegetation near NW 4th Street and Hemlock Court on July 5, which spread through a fence and burned a vacant house.

Add the Coyner Fire on top of all that, and it made for a stressful holiday weekend for a lot of Redmond neighbors.

*It's all figure-out-able, even after a weekend like that one. Here's hoping for a quieter stretch ahead.*
